papers.title: Runaway stars as progenitors of supernovae and gamma-ray bursts
papers.author_names: John J. Eldridge Norbert Langer Christopher A. Tout

abstract.text: When a core collapse supernova occurs in a binary system, the surviving star as well as the compact remnant emerging from the SN, may reach a substantial space velocity. With binary population synthesis modelling at solar and one fifth of solar metallicity, we predict the velocities of such runaway stars or binaries. We compile predictions for runaway OB stars, red supergiants and Wolf-Rayet stars. For those stars or binaries which undergo a second stellar explosion we compute their further evolution and the distance travelled until a Type II or Type Ibc SN or a long or short gamma-ray burst occurs. We find our predicted population of OB runaway stars broadly matches the observed population of stars but, to match the fastest observed WR runaway stars, we require that black holes receive an asymmetric kick upon formation. We find that at solar metallicity Type Ic SN progenitors travel shorter distances than the progenitors of other SN types because they are typically more massive and thus have shorter lifetimes. Those of Type IIP SN can fly farthest about 48 pc on average at solar metallicity. In considering the consequences of assuming that the progenitors of long GRBs are spun-up secondary stars that experience quasi-homogeneous evolution, we find that such evolution has a dramatic effect on the population of runaway WR stars and that some 30 per cent of GRBs could occur a hundred parsecs or more from their initial positions. We also consider mergers of double compact object binaries consisting of neutron stars and/or black holes. We find the most common type of visible mergers are neutron star--black hole mergers that are roughly ten times more common than neutron star--neutron star mergers. We also find that there may be a population of low-velocity neutron stars that are ejected from a binary rather than by their own natal kick.

brief.tldr: [research paper] Binary population synthesis models predict that runaway stars and binaries—formed via binary supernova ejections—travel significant distances before exploding as supernovae or gamma-ray bursts. At solar metallicity, Type IIP progenitors travel farthest (average ~48 pc, 8% reaching 100 pc), while Type Ic progenitors travel shorter distances due to higher masses and shorter lifetimes. Asymmetric kicks from black hole formation are required to match observed high-velocity Wolf-Rayet runaways. Quasi-homogeneous evolution of spun-up secondary stars in long gamma-ray burst progenitor binaries increases the fraction of GRBs occurring >100 pc from initial positions by ~30%. Neutron star–black hole mergers dominate compact binary mergers (10× more common than NS–NS), with merger times spanning years to gigayears and occurring ~300× less frequently than supernovae in the Milky Way. A population of low-velocity neutron stars ejected from binaries—rather than via natal kicks—is also predicted, implying observational kick distributions must account for binary ejection mechanisms.

source_fields.title: Runaway stars as progenitors of supernovae and gamma-ray bursts

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: At solar metallicity, our models predict that 10–30% of O stars and 2–10% of B stars are runaways with velocities ≥5 km s⁻¹, increasing to 20–30% for WR stars, with SMC-like metallicity reducing these fractions, and the inclusion of DES runaways slightly elevating the runaway fractions across all stellar types. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel analysis of how binary supernova-ejected runaway stars alter the spatial distribution of supernovae in galaxies, addressing a critical gap in progenitor studies by accounting for the non-uniform propagation of high-velocity binaries that can mislead SN distribution interpretations. Computational Method: The paper proposes a modified binary population synthesis method using detailed stellar evolution models to estimate runaway star velocities and their impact on supernova distributions, focusing on enhanced mass loss from binary interactions while simplifying the binary evolution process to improve computational efficiency. Discussion \\& Conclusions: The population synthesis model predicts that 0.5–2.2% of O stars are runaways from binary star systems, with velocities consistent with observations—especially when black hole kicks are included—and shows that type II and Ib SNe travel furthest from their origins, while most compact remnants are single, with only 20% of binaries remaining bound after supernovae.

title: Runaway stars as progenitors of supernovae and gamma-ray bursts

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents binary population synthesis modeling and theoretical predictions about runaway star populations, not observational data or catalogs of actual stars.
